title: The Brave Tin Soldier
content:
There were once five and twenty tin soldiers, all brothers, for they had been made out of the same old tin spoon. They shouldered arms and looked straight before them, and they wore a splendid uniform, red and blue.

They all looked exactly alike except one, who had only one leg, for he had been made last, and there was not enough tin to finish him. But he stood as firmly upon his one leg as the others upon their two.

On the table where the tin soldiers stood, were many other playthings, but the one that attracted most attention was a pretty little paper castle. In front of the castle stood a little maiden, made of paper too.

She was a dancer, and she stretched out both her arms, and raised one of her legs so high that the tin soldier could not see it at all, and he supposed that she had only one leg like himself.

"That is the wife for me," he thought. But she was very grand, and lived in a castle.

One evening a little boy set the tin soldier in the window, and whether it was the wind or a little goblin who did it, the window flew open and out fell the soldier, down three stories to the ground below.

It began to rain, and after a while two boys came along and found the tin soldier.

"Let's make him sail!" said one. They made a paper boat and put the tin soldier aboard.

The boat sailed down the gutter, into a drain, and into a great canal. The water rushed the boat along, faster and faster.

A great fish swallowed the tin soldier! But the brave soldier lay at full length and thought of the pretty dancer.

Later that day, the fish was caught, taken to market, sold, and brought to a kitchen. When the cook cut open the fish, there was the tin soldier!

And wonder of wonders—he was back in the very same room where he had started! There was the little paper dancer, still standing on one leg.

The tin soldier looked at her and she looked at him. They never said a word to one another, but they both knew they were meant to be together.

The End.

author: Hans Christian Andersen
